text: The surgeon identifies and dissects the pubic tubercle across the midline, extending the dissection towards the Cooper ligament for a large direct hernia, and further towards the contralateral Cooper ligament.
The surgeon rolls out a direct hernia and moves the unusual fat within the Hesselbach triangle, revealing the anatomical structures in this area.

The surgeon dissects a space of at least two centimeters between the Cooper ligament and the bladder, preparing for the flat placement of the medial and inferior edge of the mesh towards the space of Retzius. This step aims to prevent mesh displacement due to bladder distension.
The surgeon dissects between the Cooper ligament and the iliac vein to visually identify the femoral orifice and check for the presence of a femoral hernia.
The surgeon dissects the indirect sac and peritoneum, separating them from the surrounding tissue to parietalize the quartz element.
The surgeon continues dissection until the quartz element lies flat within the surgical field.
The surgeon visualizes the psoas muscle and iliac vessels, ensuring clear identification of these anatomical structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon pulls the sac and peritoneum upward while carefully dissecting between the chord elements, ensuring the quartz element remains stationary and avoiding missing any tail of the sac.
The surgeon identifies and reduces a cord lipoma, which is typically located lateral to the cord elements, using careful dissection techniques.

The surgeon dissects the peritoneum lateral to the cord elements, extending the dissection laterally beyond the anterior superior spine and sweeping it back inferiorly behind the mesh's inferior border.
The surgeon places a mesh measuring at least ten by fifteen centimeters into the surgical site after completing steps one to eight.
The surgeon displays a mesh measuring sixteen by twelve centimeters, preparing it for placement in the surgical field.
